turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the court in case 16-784, Merit Management Group v. FTI Consulting.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The Bankruptcy Code provides trustees avoiding powers to set aside and recover certain transfers for the benefit of the bankruptcy estate. It also sets out limits on the exercise of those avoiding powers. Central here is the Securities Safe Harbor, Title 11 USC Section 546(e), which provides in relevant part that the trustee may not avoid a transfer that is a covered securities transaction made by or to or for the benefit of a financial institution or other covered entities. In this case respondent FTI Consulting, the bankruptcy trustee, sought to avoid a transfer from debtor Valley View to petitioner Merit Management. The transfer was accomplished via two financial institutions; Credit Suisse and Citizens Bank. Valley View arranged for Credit Suisse to transfer funds to Citizens Bank, acting as escrow agent, and Citizens Bank then transferred the funds to Merit. Merit argued that Section 546(e) barred FTI from avoiding the transfer in light of the involvement of those financial institutions. The District Court agreed with Merit, but the Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit reversed. The question before the Court is when considering the Section 546 Safe Harbor should courts look only to the end to end transfer that the trustee seeks to avoid or should courts instead look also to any component parts of that transfer. The Court holds that the relevant transfer to consider in the Section 546(e) Safe Harbor inquiry is the transfer that the trustee seeks to avoid, here the Valley View to Merit transfer. Statutory language and context support our conclusion. The provision provides a Safe Harbor notwithstanding the avoiding powers and except for cases involving certain avoidable transfers, the heading of Section 546 is "limitations on avoiding powers". The phrase that trustee may not avoid parallels language used in the avoidance provisions and the Safe Harbor specifies that the transfer that is safe from avoidance is one that is a covered securities transaction, meaning one that itself meets the Safe Harbor criteria. Statutory structure also supports our reading of Section 546(e). The code establishes a system for avoiding transfers as well as a Safe Harbor from avoidance. It is only logical to view the pertinent transfer under Section 546(e) as the same transfer that the trustee seeks to avoid pursuant to one of its avoiding powers. Merit points to little in the text or history of the statute that supports its preferred reading of Section 546(e). For these and other reasons discussed in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit, the decision of the Court is unanimous.
